summaries.headline: Top Universities Formalize Human-Centered AI Partnership
summaries.summary: ETH Zurich, EPFL, and Stanford's Human-Centered AI Institute signed a formal agreement during the World Economic Forum to coordinate research on open foundation models and human-AI collaboration. The partnership, operating through Switzerland's National AI Institute, will focus on developing open-source models, creating evaluation benchmarks, and conducting joint research projects with researcher exchanges between institutions. The collaboration positions academic institutions to counter commercially-driven AI development with research grounded in human values and responsible deployment standards.

summaries.headline: AI Startups Shift From Speed to Workflow Design
summaries.summary: OpenAI's startup lead Laura Modiano told attendees at AI House Davos that AI literacy requirements now extend beyond technical teams to include product, sales, and operations roles across organizations. She argued that speed alone no longer provides competitive advantage, with real impact coming from redesigning entire workflows rather than automating individual tasks. The shift suggests education technology companies may need to rethink their approach to AI integration, moving from point solutions to comprehensive system redesigns that change learning outcomes rather than simply reducing costs.

summaries.headline: New York Senator Calls Electric Bus Mandate "Largest Unfunded Mandate Ever"
summaries.summary: State Senator Tom O'Mara warned that New York's all-electric school bus mandate will become the state's largest unfunded mandate during budget hearings on Governor Hochul's proposed education spending. The Republican senator noted that nearly 70% of districts—primarily rural upstate schools—face just a 1% increase in Foundation Aid that won't cover rising energy costs, much less electric bus purchases that aren't eligible for state building aid reimbursement. Education officials acknowledged there's "no black and white answer" about what happens if district voters reject electric bus funding, highlighting uncertainty around a policy that could force property tax increases in communities already losing residents to high costs.

summaries.headline: Wisconsin Governor Proposes School Funding, Tax Relief From Surplus
summaries.summary: Wisconsin Governor Tony Evers wants to use the state's $4 billion budget surplus to fund schools and provide $1.3 billion in property tax relief, as property taxes rose nearly 8 percent in 2025. Republican legislative leaders argue the surplus represents one-time money the state cannot afford to spend, with Senate President Mary Felzkowski suggesting districts should "tighten their belts" instead. The standoff reflects a familiar tension between immediate fiscal relief and long-term budget sustainability, with districts caught between rising costs and constrained revenue streams.

summaries.headline: Librarians Push for Central Role in AI Literacy
summaries.summary: School librarians are positioning themselves as natural leaders for AI literacy instruction, leveraging their existing expertise in research skills, media literacy, and technology integration. Toronto Metropolitan University has launched library-led AI workshops that teach students responsible use of AI tools, with participants earning certification badges for completing the program. The initiative highlights how librarians' core competencies align with the critical thinking skills students need to navigate AI's capabilities and limitations across academic disciplines.

summaries.headline: LEO Academy Trust Demonstrates Live EdTech Integration
summaries.summary: LEO Academy Trust showcased classroom-ready digital integration through live teaching demonstrations at BETT 2026, with a Year 6 class using real-time assessment platforms and digital manipulatives during an actual math lesson on mixed numbers. Students moved fluidly between digital tools like Learning By Questions for instant feedback and Polypad for visual modeling, while maintaining choice in how they approached tasks—some working directly on devices, others using traditional whiteboards before submitting digitally. The demonstration emphasized pedagogy-first implementation where technology supports rather than drives instruction, offering education leaders a practical model for blending digital tools with established teaching methods.

summaries.headline: Arkansas District Lands $10,000 for Early Literacy Push
summaries.summary: Riverview School District received a $10,000 grant from the Arkansas Community Foundation to purchase new instructional materials and strengthen literacy programming for elementary students. The White County Community Foundation reports early literacy has become a major funding priority as it works with school districts across the region to boost reading achievement. The investment reflects growing recognition that foundational reading skills drive both immediate academic outcomes and longer-term workforce preparation.

summaries.headline: Private School Choice Programs Resist Academic Evaluation
summaries.summary: Researchers attempting to study the academic outcomes of education savings accounts — which provide parents stipends for private education — are hitting walls as states decline to require standardized testing or share data. RAND Corporation abandoned plans for an ESA achievement study after Arizona's universal program required no testing and Florida wouldn't share its private school assessment data, despite the programs' rapid expansion across states. The evaluation gap leaves parents shopping in an increasingly crowded education marketplace without objective school performance data, while policymakers expand programs with limited evidence of their academic impact.

summaries.headline: Former Flipkart CTO Launches AI STEM Tutoring Platform
summaries.summary: Former Flipkart CTO Peeyush Ranjan has launched Fermi.ai, an AI-driven platform targeting high school STEM education in India and the US. The Singapore-based startup, co-founded with Myntra's Mukesh Bansal, focuses on mathematics, physics, and chemistry through personalized learning paths that emphasize "productive struggle" over quick answers. The platform's approach of building deeper conceptual understanding through step-by-step problem-solving reflects a broader industry shift toward AI tools that prioritize learning outcomes over convenience.

summaries.headline: Fairfax County Deploys Wearable Emergency Alert System
summaries.summary: Fairfax County Public Schools has rolled out a new emergency management system that equips staff with wearable Emergency Response Buttons and installs sensor networks throughout school buildings. The Raptor Technologies system sends immediate alerts to the district's Security Operations Center, local emergency services, and designated school staff when activated, while sensors help pinpoint the exact location of incidents within buildings. The technology serves as an additional layer beyond 911 calls, designed for situations where making a phone call might be unsafe or impractical, potentially influencing how other large districts approach comprehensive emergency response infrastructure.

summaries.headline: Ex-Flipkart CTO Launches AI Tutor That Won't Give Answers
summaries.summary: Peeyush Ranjan, former CTO of Flipkart and ex-Google executive, has launched Fermi.ai, an AI-powered STEM tutoring platform that deliberately refuses to provide direct answers to student questions. The Singapore-based startup guides high school students through problem-solving processes instead, addressing what Ranjan calls a growing trend of students using AI tools like ChatGPT to bypass actual learning—evidenced by noticeable drops in ChatGPT traffic during summer breaks when schools close. The platform's approach of maintaining "productive struggle" positions it as a counter-response to concerns that AI homework assistance is weakening student comprehension and cognitive development.

summaries.headline: High School Mental Health Support Gains Student Trust
summaries.summary: Monta Vista High School students show significantly increased comfort seeking help from counselors and teachers, according to multi-year survey data tracking wellness indicators. The percentage of students comfortable asking counselors for help "often" or "always" jumped from 28% in 2018-19 to 58% in 2024-25, while comfort approaching teachers rose from 56% to 71% over the same period. The trends suggest schools can measurably shift student attitudes toward mental health support through sustained wellness programming, creating pathways for earlier intervention when students face academic or personal challenges.

summaries.headline: Meta Launches AI Glasses Grants for Schools and Accessibility
summaries.summary: Meta announced a new grants program targeting U.S. organizations using AI glasses for accessibility, education, and social impact applications. The AI Glasses Impact Grants include both Accelerator Grants for organizations already implementing the technology and Catalyst Grants for early-stage projects, with priority areas including skills development, health, and community support. The initiative reflects growing evidence that hands-free, context-aware AI tools are finding practical applications in educational and accessibility settings where traditional screen-based technology creates barriers.

summaries.headline: Pearson Partners with Indian State on AI Skills
summaries.summary: Pearson signed an agreement with India's Telangana state government to develop AI education and credentialing through Aikam, the state's innovation hub. The partnership will combine Pearson's assessment expertise with Telangana's policy leadership to build what they call a "Global AI Academy" aimed at creating internationally portable AI credentials. The collaboration signals how education companies are looking beyond traditional markets to establish footholds in emerging AI workforce development, particularly in regions positioning themselves as global tech talent hubs.

summaries.headline: Consultants Tell West Virginia to Cap Private Scholarships
summaries.summary: RAND Corporation analysts told West Virginia lawmakers the state should cap its Hope Scholarship program based on family income while better funding public schools through a less convoluted formula. The Hope Scholarship now consumes more than $230 million—75% of the state education department's operating budget—as the state simultaneously took over Hancock County schools for inability to make payroll. Education advocates argue districts like Hancock had to rely on temporary COVID funds to maintain staff because the school aid formula provides insufficient support while private scholarships drain resources from public systems.

summaries.headline: LEGO Education Pushes Beyond AI Tool Training
summaries.summary: LEGO Education used its Bett 2026 keynote to argue that AI literacy should focus on understanding how systems work rather than teaching students to use AI tools as "magic boxes." The company partnered with LEO Academy Trust educators who reported that while students approach AI with confidence, they lack comprehension of underlying concepts, leading classrooms to shift from "black box" to "glass box" thinking about AI mechanics. The emphasis on foundational understanding over tool proficiency signals a potential shift in how districts approach AI curriculum as they balance rising expectations with educator readiness concerns.

summaries.headline: Education Leaders Must Prove AI Impact, Not Just Adoption
summaries.summary: Education technology has moved beyond piloting AI features to requiring demonstrated proof that artificial intelligence improves learning outcomes at scale. The shift means districts and publishers must answer what improved, for whom, and under what conditions rather than simply showcasing AI capabilities like chat interfaces or automated feedback. Product teams now face pressure to treat efficacy as a core design requirement with clear success criteria and continuous improvement loops, fundamentally changing how AI tools are developed and evaluated in schools.

summaries.headline: FDA Nutrition Labels May Miss Lower-Literacy Consumers
summaries.summary: Researchers tested the FDA's proposed "Nutrition Info Box" front-of-package labels against consumer nutrition literacy levels as the agency finalizes labeling requirements for most packaged foods. The study found that while the proposed labels worked best for consumers with higher nutrition literacy, they may not effectively guide food choices for all Americans across education and income levels. School nutrition directors and health educators face a policy tool that could leave behind the students and families who most need support making healthier food choices.

summaries.headline: Pennsylvania Districts See Results from Post-Lawsuit Funding Increases
summaries.summary: Pennsylvania lawmakers visited William Penn School District Tuesday to highlight the impact of increased state funding following a 2023 court ruling that declared the state's school funding system unconstitutional. The district received $80 million in the current budget—roughly $10 million more than the previous year—with Superintendent Eric Becoats reporting that eight of 11 elementary schools met or exceeded growth expectations and the ninth-grade academy's building score nearly doubled from 56 to 95 over two years. The symbolic visit to the district that led the landmark lawsuit comes as lawmakers prepare for the next budget cycle, with advocates positioning the funding increases as evidence that sustained investment produces measurable academic gains.

summaries.headline: Aldine ISD Showcases Five-Year Science of Reading Journey
summaries.summary: Aldine Independent School District will host its sixth annual Literacy Matters Conference on February 28, a free virtual event highlighting the district's sustained commitment to research-based reading instruction since 2019. The Houston-area district recorded the largest reading gains among large Texas districts on the spring 2025 STAAR assessment, following its adoption of the Amplify Core Knowledge Language Arts model and implementation of structured literacy practices even during the pandemic. The conference positions Aldine as a proof point for districts seeking evidence that consistent implementation of science of reading principles can yield measurable results in diverse student populations.

# When art and literacy go hand in hand: Moffat County Library teams with Sandrock educator, students to showcase artwork and spark interest in reading

[News](https://www.craigdailypress.com/recent-stories/news) \| Jan 26, 2026

![John Camponeschi](https://swiftmedia.s3.amazonaws.com/mountain.swiftcom.com/images/sites/10/2025/01/05200932/IMG_2633-150x150.jpg)

###### [John Camponeschi](https://www.craigdailypress.com/profile/john-camponeschi/)

[jcamponeschi@craigdailypress.com](mailto:jcamponeschi@craigdailypress.com)

Share this story Share

Share on FacebookShare on TwitterShare on LinkedInShare on WhatsAppShare via EmailShare via SMSCopy Link

![](https://swiftmedia.s3.amazonaws.com/mountain.swiftcom.com/images/sites/10/2026/01/25162357/6a601c05-0584-43a8-b368-897e4544162f-1024x768.jpeg)**Moffat County Library services supervisor Tracy Spencer said many of the students who visited the library to hang their art had never been there before. Spencer and Michelle Parker, an art educator and math interventionist at Sandrock Elementary, hope future collaborations can continue to spark interest in the library for students and their families.**

_Moffat County School District/Courtesy Photo_

A wall at Craig’s Moffat County Library has become a winter-themed gallery resulting from a partnership that sparked by donated art supplies and grew into a student incentive, a library lesson and a push to connect families with local resources.

The display features artwork created by Michelle Parker’s students at Sandrock Elementary School, where she teaches K-5 art and serves as a math interventionist. Parker said the project started when she visited the library looking for affordable ideas for art lessons.

While checking out books, Parker talked with a librarian named Lynn, who mentioned she could help obtain materials. A few days later, Parker said she and her students received an early Christmas present in the form of watercolor paper and paints delivered to the school.

* * *

* * *

To return the gesture, Parker created a monthlong incentive for fifth graders in December called “Art, Heart and Creativity.” The goal was to celebrate creativity while encouraging students to take better care of classroom materials and put extra effort into their work.

Parker said students earned points each day throughout December for positive behavior and strong participation in the art room. The top point earners received an opportunity that was part reward and part field trip, visiting the library to hang artwork, enjoy a scavenger hunt and complete a small craft activity arranged by library staff.

* * *

* * *

![](https://swiftmedia.s3.amazonaws.com/mountain.swiftcom.com/images/sites/10/2026/01/25162344/f9a76b30-026a-4610-8cc5-bf9a3c86df09-1024x768.jpeg)Michelle Parker stands with her students during a visit to the Moffat County Library. The students were there to celebrate their artwork while also learning about the library and all it has to offer.Courtesy Photo/Moffat County School District

“It was really fun,” Parker said. “I watched them really start to put their all into their art and kind of step up their game a little bit.”

#### From art display to first library visits

Library services supervisor Tracy Spencer said Parker contacted the library with the idea of sharing student art with the public.

“I thought it was a great idea,” Spencer said. “We found an empty wall space here in the library that would be a great place to display the art.”

Once the students arrived, staff used the moment to introduce the building and its services to children who, in some cases, had never been inside the library before.

“Out of those 11 children, only two of them had been to the library before,” Spencer said. “Only one of them had a library card.”

Spencer said that pattern is common during school tours and special visits. The art show offered a natural conduit because students were excited to see their work displayed while also being eager to explore the library’s spaces.

“We wanted to really introduce the library to these kids and show what a cool place it is and all the cool books that we have,” Spencer said.

#### A treasure hunt with practical skills

During the visit, Spencer said students participated in a short lesson on using the library’s digital catalog. Each student chose a topic, searched it on a computer, selected a title and went to find the book on the shelves. Spencer described it as both a scavenger hunt and a “book treasure hunt,” which resulted in students racing between computers and the stacks.

![](https://swiftmedia.s3.amazonaws.com/mountain.swiftcom.com/images/sites/10/2026/01/25162332/ae5ce35b-ab6f-4c87-aff8-a682baaa9fec-768x1024.jpeg)Sandrock students used their time at the library to learn how to search the library’s catalog for books that interested them.Courtesy Photo/Moffat County School District

“They had a lot of fun doing it,” Spencer said. “Some of them did it so quickly that they wanted to go find another book.”

Parker said the experience mattered because many of the students who earned the trip were not regular library visitors.

“They were very excited and they wanted to get library cards,” Parker said. “They were talking about wanting to go back.”

Parker said the visit also helped students see how the library can support school and personal interests. She sees art and literacy as connected through research, storytelling and language that can inspire creative work.

“I really feel like art and literacy go hand in hand,” Parker said. “You kind of can’t have one without the other.”

#### Pride and motivation in the classroom

Parker said she encourages students through projects that tap into their interests. She said students often gain confidence when they can see their improvement across multiple attempts before completing a final piece.

She said the display of their finished works matters as well. At Sandrock, student artwork is hung throughout the school. Parker noted that she often sees children pointing out their pieces in the hallway to friends and classmates.

“That motivates them,” she said. “It helps them foster that sense of pride in their own work.”

Student projects this year have included second grade perspective drawings and mixed-media, three-dimensional works. Fifth graders used watercolor and oil pastels to learn watercolor resist techniques and create festive nutcrackers, while kindergarten students focused on developing scissor skills through cutting projects.

#### A partnership with room to grow

Spencer said the art show is one way the library tries to reach families who do not regularly walk through its doors. She emphasized that the library is free, library cards are free and families can visit without checking out books.

“They can come in and just enjoy the library, pull books off the shelf, sit down and read them with their kids,” she said.

Spencer said she suspects time pressures and busy schedules play a role in families not making it to the library as often as they would like, especially in households where both parents work. She said the library tries to reduce barriers with evening hours, Fridays when school is not in session and the first and third Saturday of the month.

Despite making the library as accessible as possible, Spencer said it at times takes a catalyst to bring in new families. With the Sandrock display, she said that was as simple as a child telling a parent that their artwork was being shown at the library.

Both Parker and Spencer said they want the collaboration to continue, and Spencer said the library and Sandrock plan to refresh the display seasonally. Another set of pieces is expected later in the winter.

Parker also said she hopes the partnership can expand, both to continue celebrating student creativity and to introduce more children to the library’s resources and reading programs.

“I’d like to see some of the other schools in town get involved,” Parker said.

For Spencer, the objective behind youth programming is to help children enjoy books and reading in a self-motivated way.

“We just want kids to know that reading is fun,” Spencer said. “It’s not homework.”

For the students whose art now hangs on the library’s walls, that lesson came with a new sense of pride and a new familiarity with shelves that hold the gateways to blossoming minds and brighter futures.

More information about the Moffat County Library is available at MoffatCounty.Colorado.gov/Services/Library.

local_articles.title: TCTC Initiative Promotes Literacy, Career Exposure
local_articles.summary: TCTC Initiative Promotes Literacy, Career Exposure  Business Journal Daily
local_articles.full_content:
[Skip to content](https://businessjournaldaily.com/tctc-initiative-promotes-literacy-career-exposure/#main)

[Home](https://businessjournaldaily.com/) » TCTC Initiative Promotes Literacy, Career Exposure

![](https://businessjournaldaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/TCTC-program1.jpg)

- Share this story!
- [![Share Facebook](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://businessjournaldaily.com/tctc-initiative-promotes-literacy-career-exposure/)
- [![Share LinkedIn](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://businessjournaldaily.com/tctc-initiative-promotes-literacy-career-exposure/)
- [![Share Twitter/X](<Base64-Image-Removed>)](https://x.com/intent/post?url=https://businessjournaldaily.com/tctc-initiative-promotes-literacy-career-exposure/&text=TCTC%20Initiative%20Promotes%20Literacy,%20Career%20Exposure)

CHAMPION, Ohio – A Trumbull Career & Technical Center program helps elementary students learn literacy skills and gain career exposure and provides high school students with work-based learning.

Meghan Mock, a TCTC English teacher and co-chair of the school’s literacy committee, and Kris Doran, the school’s interactive multimedia instructor, developed [TCTC Tales](https://www.tctchome.com/page/tctc-tales), a [literacy initiative](https://businessjournaldaily.com/tctc-launches-literacy-initiative-to-inspire-young-readers/) that combines videos and hands-on classroom activities based on children’s books. Each book focuses on a career field for which TCTC offers a program.

“The books aren’t just any book, but it’s a specific link to the programs that we serve, that we want to promote,” Mock said.

The first seven installments of TCTC Tales – fire and EMS; cosmetology; exercise science and sports medicine; biotechnology; construction technology; welding; and dental assisting – were delivered to elementary schools in member districts in November. More episodes are in the works, with plans for a video for each TCTC program.

TCTC received a state grant a few years ago that funded the program’s development, and Mock said the lessons were designed around the Science of Reading, the literacy curriculum required in Ohio.

In each video, Sam Stephens, a TCTC computer technician, visits the school’s program, meets its students and asks questions about the profession. Young viewers then learn vocabulary words associated with the featured career and introduced by TCTC students. Lesson plans and extension activities accompany each video.

Mock, working with TCTC’s literacy team, found books to align with every program at TCTC except Doran’s. But the interactive multimedia instructor has a solution.

“In the spring, we’re going to attempt to write our own book and get it published,” he said.

Students in Doran’s class read the children’s books, visited each program, talked to the students and instructors, wrote the video scripts and determined what to videorecord to include in each episode.

Seniors Jaxson Bell and Kierstin Richoz are working on videos highlighting more TCTC programs.

“I’m doing more of a behind the scenes aspect, where I’m kind of showcasing the students and the teachers and everyone who’s putting the effort and the work to put it together,” Richoz said.

It’s a documentary-style video, separate from those that feature each TCTC program, and will be added to the TCTC Tales [website](https://www.tctchome.com/page/tctc-tales), informing viewers about the work involved in the series.

Bell is working on the next round of TCTC Tales.

“I’m doing the production of the episodes themselves, what they show to kids and stuff of that sort, as well as the editing and general production,” he said. “I have to manage my crew, get all the shots that I need and then, after the fact, go and edit it together and make a fully fledged episode.”

Senior Shaun Sudol is a student in TCTC’s app and web development program.

![](https://businessjournaldaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/TCTC-program2.jpg)Trumbull Career & Technical Center students Kierstin Richoz and Jaxson Bell, who are part of the interactive multimedia program, and Shaun Sudol, who is in app and game development, work on TCTC Tales, a series of videos and lessons that promote literacy and career awareness to elementary school students.

“I’m developing the games that are going to be played by the elementary students,” he said.

Games that align with the featured TCTC programs will be added to the website and reinforce what the children learn in the videos. Sudol has completed five games. They’re a later TCTC Tales addition.

And gamification is an effective learning tool, Doran explained.

While the work the TCTC students are doing amounts to lessons for first through third graders, they’re learning too.

“The best way to execute everything is to make sure everyone knows what they’re doing and make sure everyone has a timeline of when things need to be done,” Richoz said.

Doran models the assignments on work-based learning so students learn what it’s like working as part of a professional crew.

After graduation, Bell plans to study film and media arts at Cleveland State University. He wants to pursue a career in film, television or marketing.

“It’s definitely more hands-on than most other jobs out there, as well as it simulates a part of my brain that doesn’t necessarily get used in other conventional jobs, like a nine-to-five,” he said.

Richoz plans to major in multimedia communications at Youngstown State University and wants to work in film or television, ideally as a producer or director.

“But I want to work with stop-motion animation and stuff like that,” she said. “I want to make things, and I want to kind of do what I’m doing now, where I’m making sure everyone has their own space and we can put things together and create projects.”

Sudol intends to enroll in computer design or software engineering school.

Bell and Richoz will present TCTC Tales on Feb. 4 in a student showcase at the Ohio Career Technical Education Legislative Conference in Columbus.

The program has fans closer to home too.

Carla Collins, a second grade teacher at Blott Guy PK-6 School in Liberty, started the books with her students before the holiday break as part of social studies instruction.

“These lessons were really, really well-planned-out,” Collins said. “They directly follow what we’ve learned. We’ve had a lot of professional development on the Science of Reading, and so they directly align with what we’ve learned.”

Collins takes her students to TCTC annually, allowing them to learn about careers with which they may be unfamiliar.

While Collins piloted TCTC Tales with her students, other second grade teachers at the school will roll it out to their classes. She recommends it. Collins invited parents and community members in the career fields highlighted by the lessons to visit her class.

Her students watched the video at the beginning of each lesson, which introduced each career field before they read the books.

![](https://businessjournaldaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/TCTC-program3.jpg)Carla Collins, a second grade teacher at Blott Guy PK-6 School in Liberty, and her students Micah Bond, Esme Ludwig, Violet Higley, Camille Rhoden, Lorenzo Gonzalez and Braden Hamerik studied TCTC Tales in class.

Second graders Esme Ludwig, Violet Higley, Micah Bond, Lorenzo Gonzalez, Braden Hamerik and Camille Rhoden gave the lessons high marks.

Esme pointed to something she recalled from the biotechnology lesson: “That germs can help you and make you sick,” she said.

Camille liked learning about germs too.

Micah enjoyed the lessons on biotechnology, firefighting and construction technology. And Violet learned how to be safe in the firefighting lesson and how to take care of herself in exercise science and sports medicine.

Braden enjoyed the construction technology and welding lessons in particular, while Lorenzo liked exercise science because he plays a lot of sports.

The TCTC teachers hope other career technical centers can learn from TCTC Tales and design similar programs. Doran said the lessons are designed so elementary school teachers can use whatever best suits their classes.

“I think the beauty of it is, it’s all there and it’s completely up to them,” Doran said. “… There’s eventually going to be 30 different programs to pick from 30 different books.”

_Pictured at top: Meghan Mock and Kris Doran of Trumbull Career & Technical Center._

BELOIT — The first of two meetings this week by the School District of Beloit Board of Education involving a proposed pilot literacy program that would be funded by the Hendricks Family Foundation again focused on unanimity.

On Dec. 15, Hendricks Family Foundation Executive Director Lisa Furseth told the school board that the foundation wanted to fund a $2.5 million, three-year pilot literacy program at one of the district’s elementary schools.

The offer was a surprise because the foundation, which is the charitable arm of ABC Supply co-founder Diane Hendricks and her family, is the primary backer of The Lincoln Academy public charter school.

Furseth said the foundation wanted to move quickly and launch the program at one Beloit elementary school in the fall but had some conditions. One was the foundation wanted to get unanimous backing by the school board.

At the last school board meeting on Jan. 6, which was the first time the board spoke publicly about the idea, two school board members, Amiee Leavy and Megan Miller expressed reservations. On Monday, Furseth came back to the school board to expand upon the idea and take questions from the board.

Leavy and Miller again appeared to not be fully on board.

Leavy asked Furseth why a 7-0 vote to do the program was so important considering the makeup of the board is going to change in April and the next board will be hiring a permanent superintendent and new director of teaching and learning.

Furseth mentioned that the foundation had funded a three-year pilot program back in 2014 that paid for full-time career advocate to help create career pathways at Beloit Memorial High School. The school district ended up ending that program early.

“A no vote isn’t neutral, it’s opposition,” Furseth said. “Opposition does have an impact on whether or not people are supportive, especially when the work gets hard and this will get hard.”

Miller, who unlike Leavy is not up for re-election in April, had a number of reservations. She asked Furseth why an online tutoring program would be better than the district investing in a recruitment program to find local volunteers to come in and tutor kids.

Furseth said the tutoring part of the proposal, which would be provided by Pennsylvania-based OpenLiteracy, would be easier to implement and would be easier to expand if successful.

“(A community-based volunteer program) is great, but it’s a really hard lift,” Furseth said. “You not only have to recruit the volunteers, you have to train them and provide supervision to make sure they are there on a day-to-day basis. We didn’t think we were in a position to come to you and say ‘we have this great idea, but it’s a heavy lift.’”

Later, Miller asked how the foundation could launch a literacy program at a Beloit school considering the district already has a shortage of paraprofessionals, which would be necessary to supervise the students. Miller also asked if the foundation would be willing to delay the idea while the district worked through its staffing and leadership issues.

“If we want to really partner, I think we need to pause and do a needs assessment and do a strategic plan,” Miller said.

Furseth did not have an immediate answer

“If your question is, is there room to negotiate on the timeline, my answer is ‘I don’t know,’” Furseth said. “That’s a conversation we would need to have.”

Furseth urged the board not to delay.

“One of the things about schools is that time runs in annual cycles,” Furseth said. “A delay is a year. It’s not a month, a couple of months. It’s a year ... In the meantime, the people paying the price is our children.”

Carol Fox, Brian Nichols and Tia Johnson expressed support for the idea. JoAnne, a former teacher in the Beloit district who now teaches in Janesville, only had reservations about staff bonuses and how they would be implemented. Tom Hankins did not make the meeting.

The district scheduled a special meeting for Thursday for the board to meet with representatives from OpenLiteracy and California-based Skyrocket Education, which would provide onsite leadership and culture training at whatever Beloit elementary school was picked.

Kurth Memorial Library will host free financial literacy classes at noon each Tuesday in February. Joe Ceasar, executive director of Legacy Institute for Financial Education, will lead the classes, which are open to the public. No registration is required.

Ceasar said that people need to know how to make their money work for them.

“In our society, we have higher rates of depression, we have higher rates of just general unhappiness, because of a perception that they don't have enough to survive on, and that perception is largely driven by their lack of financial literacy,” Ceasar said.

The classes will establish a base of financial knowledge that will help people make better decisions concerning money.

Understanding budgets will be a key point in the series. Participants will learn the basics of stewardship, how to create a budget and how to stay within it. The importance of a financial reputation, or credit score, will be covered, as well as debt, lending and interest rates. The last lesson will teach about investing and giving.

“I have clients that believe a budget is just accounting for what has happened versus telling their money what to do,” he said. “So their finances control them.”

He said that mishandling money can harm relationships with people, including spouses and children.

“The number one reason for a divorce in America is because of finances and a concept called financial adultery,” Ceasar said.

The purpose of Legacy Institute is to educate people about finances so that the next generation is better prepared for the future.

“We go out and do workshops for quite a few people in organizations as far as Houston, Dallas and obviously here in Lufkin,” Ceasar said. “And from both walks of life, people who are spirit-driven and people who are not, they both come back with the same types of comments.”

Participants often express the wish that they had learned earlier how to manage money.

“People frequently make those types of comments, especially when they learn about the power of compound interest and the benefits of starting early,” he said.

The reason Ceasar’s organization is called Legacy Institute is because he believes that he has an obligation to leave the next generation a better experience. The goal is to help each generation improve through financial literacy.

“When most people think about legacies, they think about financial resources or houses or cars or even a reputation,” Ceasar said. “But we believe that knowledge is also part of your legacy.”

Households with low to moderate income that do a good job of teaching their children financial literacy will have better outcomes later on in life, he said. A lot of kids come back from school because they don't know how to manage money, so that lack of stewardship hurts them at a collegiate level.

Students with financial awareness will be more likely to stay in college because they will know how to manage their Pell grants and their scholarships.

He said it is important to teach children how to manage finances.

“Proverbs chapter 22 and six says, if you train up a child in the way he should go, when he is older, he won't depart from it,” Ceasar said. “But the very next verse says, the rich rules over the poor and the borrower is servant to the lender.”

The classes aim to provide knowledge that will form a solid base for financial management.

“I would say of all the people that we meet maybe be 10% actually have it down before we meet,” Ceasar said. “I consider myself a lifelong student, so you can always learn something.”

“I believe giving is a privilege and that privilege is earned by sound financial management,” Ceasar said.

Classes are for all ages and levels of knowledge.

“A lot of people, as they get older, they think it's too late for them. The best time to know this stuff is 20 years ago, but the next best time is now,” Ceasar said.

The Riverview School District has received a $10,000 grant from the Arkansas Community Foundation “for their early literacy focus to raise their scores and purchase new curriculum and training for it,” according to Denise Eaves, executive director of the White County Community Foundation.

The grant is part of more than $1 million being awarded to nonprofits across the state as the Arkansas Community Foundation celebrates its 50th anniversary.

Eaves said the grant check for Riverview was received by Assistant Superintendent Susan Blockburger and White County was “mapped in with all of central Arkansas for those grants.”

“The White County Community Foundation has a huge push to assist school districts and nonprofits that are addressing early literacy,” Eaves said, “and due to Aspire reports, White County has scores that need improvements in early literacy/third-grade reading, so we were very happy to recommend and grant them with the $10,000 grant due to their continued efforts and focus.”

Jessica Ford, president and chief executive officer of the Arkansas Community Foundation, said that the organization wanted to celebrate its 50th year “by no party, no initiative, but by making grants to the nonprofits that are out there doing the work statewide.”

“If you lay a tic-tac-toe grid over the state of Arkansas, you hit nine regions,” Ford said, “and we looked at data in all those nine regions to determine what are the biggest needs in those areas, and we found the nonprofits who were addressing those and we granted to them.

“We gave $50,000 to organizations that have been around for a really long time – we call those our legacy nonprofits – and then we gave $30,000 to organizations that are a little bit newer – we call those the rising nonprofits and for our affiliate network. We have 29 affiliates all across the state. We made grants in the size of $10,000 and we let the local boards determine what those grants would look like.”

Ford said [arcf.org/50](http://arcf.org/50) can be visited to see all three tiers of these nonprofits. Visitors can click on the button that says affiliates and see the statewide network. She said north-central and central Arkansas organizations are well represented.
